locked_facts: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu² (net work equals change in kinetic energy)
Work done by a constant force: W = Fd cos(θ)
Work done by a variable force: W = ∫F·dx (area under F-x curve)
Work done by gravity on object moving height h downward: W = mgh, upward: W = -mgh
Work done by friction is always negative: W_friction = -f·d
At constant velocity: W_net = 0, therefore ΔKE = 0
Work-energy theorem is valid for both constant and variable forces
truth_statements: The net work done on a body equals the change in its kinetic energy, regardless of the nature of forces
Work done by a force is the dot product of force and displacement vectors: W = F·d = Fd cos(θ)
For a variable force, work equals the area under the force-displacement graph
When multiple forces act, the net work is the algebraic sum of work done by each force individually
The work-energy theorem is a scalar equation — it relates magnitudes, not directions

aha_moment.what_to_show: A block sliding on a rough surface with F applied — the F-x graph shows area under curve equals ΔKE numerically. Work bars for applied force (positive) and friction (negative) sum to give net work matching ΔKE readout exactly.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Graph area shaded = ΔKE displayed numerically. W_net bar and ΔKE bar are identical height, proving the theorem.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that only NET work matters — applied work alone does not equal ΔKE when friction is present

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Block of mass m on a surface. Applied force F and friction f shown as arrows. F-x graph axes visible but empty. KE readout shows ½mu². Block has initial velocity u.
simulation_states.emphasis: Establish the initial KE and all forces — applied, friction, weight, normal

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Block moves under net force. F-x graph traces the applied force curve. Shaded area under F-x grows as displacement increases. KE readout updates. Work bars accumulate — W_applied (green), W_friction (red).
simulation_states.emphasis: Area under F-x curve is growing — this IS the work being done

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Block reaches final position. Total shaded area under net force curve displayed alongside ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu². Both values match exactly. The W_net bar and ΔKE bar are side-by-side at equal height.
simulation_states.emphasis: W_net = ΔKE proven visually — area under net force curve = change in kinetic energy

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust applied force magnitude, friction coefficient, and initial velocity. F-x graph and KE readout update in real time. Try variable force (sinusoidal) to see ∫F·dx = ΔKE still holds.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change any parameter — the theorem W_net = ΔKE always holds. Try zero friction, try F < f.

common_misconceptions.belief: Work done by applied force alone equals the change in kinetic energy
trigger_phrases: sirf applied force se KE badhti hai
work by one force equals kinetic energy change
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT clarifies: the NET work done by ALL forces equals ΔKE. If friction also does negative work, you must subtract it. W_net = W_applied + W_friction = ΔKE.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show W_applied and W_friction bars summing to W_net, which matches ΔKE

common_misconceptions.belief: Work-energy theorem only works for constant forces
trigger_phrases: theorem doesn't work for changing force
common_misconceptions.correction: The work-energy theorem is universally valid. For variable forces, work is calculated as W = ∫F·dx (area under F-x graph), and this area still equals ΔKE exactly.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Toggle to variable force mode — area under curved F-x graph still equals ΔKE numerically

legend.meaning: Work done = area under F-x curve
Kinetic energy ½mv² at current instant
Net work done by all forces
